reasoning: All four reach the same sound verdict (decline as structured, counter-offer into a bounded bet), so discrimination comes from rigor, calibration, and bias-handling. Slot A wins decisively: it is the only response that carries explicit, internally consistent runway math through to a survival-relevant conclusion ($60k/mo burn, $400k minus ~$300k team cost = ~$100k true net plus ~$300k deferred spend), names the outside-view/base-rate move and the vivid-detail trap it guards against, applies fragility/barbell and a premortem, lists concrete falsifiers, and is the only one to attach a justified confidence (~70%) tied to the specific facts it lacks. Slot C is a close second with strong assumptions, the sharp "30 person-months" reframe, a correct sunk-cost check, and specific repricing, but gives no explicit probability and leaks a local-file-path footer. Slot D is solid and well-balanced (attribute-substitution and shifting-the-burden named, three diagnostics), but leaves burn as a "$X/month" placeholder and offers no calibrated confidence. Slot B is the weakest: good structure and a clear services-trap loop, but it never computes burn, hedges quantification ("close to break-even"), names fewer biases, and provides no explicit confidence, making it the least rigorous and least calibrated of the four.
